highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.183 Released
highlights.summary: The latest release adds important safety guardrails in auto mode: destructive git commands like `git reset --hard` and `git clean -fd` are now blocked unless you explicitly asked to discard local work. It also prevents `git commit --amend` on commits the agent didn't make during the current session, protecting your history from unintended rewrites. This is a significant safety improvement for developers who let Claude Code run autonomously.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.181 Released
highlights.summary: This release introduces the `/config key=value` syntax, letting you change settings like thinking mode directly from the prompt without editing config files (e.g. `/config thinking=false`). It works in interactive mode, headless `-p` mode, and Remote Control, making on-the-fly configuration much more convenient. macOS users also gain an opt-in `sandbox.allowAppleEvents` setting for sandboxed commands.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.178 Released
highlights.summary: This release adds the powerful `Tool(param:value)` permission rule syntax, letting you match and block specific tool calls by their input parameters — for example, `Agent(model:opus)` blocks subagents from using the expensive Opus model. Nested `.claude/skills` directories now load automatically when working in those folders, with name-clash resolution handled gracefully. These features give advanced users much finer-grained control over agent behavior and project structure.

highlights.title: Anthropic Pauses Credit Change for Claude Code
highlights.summary: Anthropic has postponed a previously announced change that would have moved Claude Agent SDK, `claude -p`, and third-party apps off subscription rate limits and onto a separate monthly credit pool. If you received an email warning about this billing change, the transition has been delayed and your current subscription usage patterns remain intact for now. Keep an eye on official communications for a rescheduled date.

highlights.title: Show HN: Claude Code for Visual Studio (native diff with accept/reject)
highlights.summary: A developer built a Claude Code extension for Microsoft Visual Studio (not VS Code) that implements the same protocol as the official IDE plugins, enabling native diff views with accept/reject buttons. This fills a gap since Anthropic only officially supports VS Code and JetBrains, making Claude Code accessible to developers still working in Visual Studio. Check the GitHub repo's GIF for a quick visual demo of the workflow.

highlights.title: From Zero to Claude Code in 19 Minutes (no code)
highlights.summary: This short YouTube tutorial walks complete beginners through getting started with Claude Code without any prior coding experience, covering setup and basic usage in under 20 minutes. It's an efficient starting point if you want a quick, hands-on overview before diving into longer courses. The 'no code' framing makes it especially approachable for non-developers exploring AI-assisted workflows.

highlights.title: Mastering Claude Code in 30 Minutes
highlights.summary: This focused 30-minute YouTube tutorial aims to take developers from basic familiarity to confident usage of Claude Code's key features. It's a good middle-ground option between quick intro clips and multi-hour full courses, ideal for developers who already know some basics and want to level up efficiently. Watching this alongside trying the `/config` and permission features from the latest releases is a great combo.

tips_of_the_day: Use `/config thinking=false` at the start of a session for simple tasks to reduce token usage and speed up responses — save thinking mode for complex architectural decisions.

Before letting Claude Code run in auto mode on a long task, make sure you have a clean `git status` first. The new v2.1.183 safety guards are great, but a committed baseline means you can always roll back cleanly no matter what.

Use `Tool(param:value)` permission rules to cap costs in multi-agent workflows — for example, add `Agent(model:opus)` to your deny list so subagents default to a cheaper model unless you explicitly override it.
